name: aether-language
description: Syntax highlighting and Language Server integration for Aether — a modern actor-based programming language.

[aether]._note: The Aether LSP emits no semantic tokens, so the fixed TextMate palette below is the sole colour source — keep it that way. Disabling semantic highlighting for .ae makes the palette authoritative on every platform even if a future toolchain or a second extension starts emitting semantic tokens (those follow the user's theme and would otherwise differ across Windows/macOS and flicker when the server reconnects).

textMateRules._design_note: Aether ships a FIXED, red-forward palette for the .aether-suffixed scopes the grammar emits, so the .ae reading experience is identical on every machine and every base theme (Windows / macOS / Linux). The user's colour theme still paints everything OUTSIDE this list (markdown, JSON, JS, etc.) and any .ae token we don't enumerate. The Aether LSP emits no semantic tokens, so these TextMate rules are authoritative — nothing overrides them. The palette is red-dominant on purpose (an Erlang-inspired identity): keywords, types, the actor verbs and the structural landmarks all live in the red family, with gold reserved for function definitions (the one anchor the eye needs to navigate) and muted green/amber/sage for comments/strings/numbers so the code stays legible rather than monochrome.
textMateRules._palette_legend: Comments=green-italic (#6A9955) | Strings=amber (#CE9178) | Numbers=sage (#B5CEA8) | Literals=red-italic (#E06C75) | Keywords=red-italic (#E06C75) | Types=salmon (#E59CA3) | Actors/Messages=red-bold (#E06C75) | Functions=gold (#DCDCAA) | Variables=slate (#ABB2BF) | Arrows/send=red-bold (#E06C75) | Operators=slate (#ABB2BF) | Brackets=dim (#5C6370)

textMateRules._role: Comments — green italic. Comments are scaffolding; legible without competing with code.
Strings — amber. Warm but clearly distinct from the red keyword/type family so a string never reads as code.
Escape sequences inside strings — mustard, a step off the string body.
String interpolation `${...}` — red, matching keywords so 'code-inside-a-string' signals 'this is code, not text'.
Numbers — sage green. Distinct from amber strings and red keywords, readable at all sizes; one colour across every base (binary / octal / hex / float).
Literal constants — `true`, `false`, `null`, `self`. Red italic: lexically irreducible atoms, the Erlang-atom hue.
Control + declaration keywords — `if`, `else`, `match`, `for`, `while`, `return`, `import`, `as`, etc. Red italic: the dominant accent, the eye's anchor in a dense line.
Storage-class keywords — `struct`, `actor`, `message`, `func`, `state`, `extern`, `let`, `var`, `const`. Same red as control keywords; structurally the same role (they introduce things).
Primitive types — `int`, `string`, `bool`, `byte`, `float`, `ptr`. Salmon: the red family, lighter than keywords so types and the keywords that introduce them stay distinguishable.
Attribute annotations — `@c_import`, `@packed`, `@derive`, `@extern`, `@c_callback`, `@heap`, `@retain`. Salmon italic: modifiers in the red family, set apart from keywords by the italic.
Actor and message-type names — red bold. They carry behavioural meaning (something receives them, something sends them); bold elevates them above inert types.
Struct and user-type names — salmon, no bold. Same family as actors/messages but less emphatic; structs are inert data.
Function definitions — gold, bold. The one non-red anchor: definitions structure a file and the eye must find them fast; gold reads cleanly against the red field.
The `main()` entry point — gold bold italic so it pops among many helpers.
Built-in / support functions — gold, no bold. Stdlib calls are tools, not structure.
Variables — slate. Deliberately neutral so the red accents and gold functions carry the colour; bindings are the canvas, not the paint.

Arrow / actor / cons operators — red bold. `->` (return type), `!`/`?` (send/ask), `|` (cons). The syntactic turning points; red-bold marks them as landmarks, in the same hue as the keywords they punctuate.
Ordinary operators — assignment, comparison, logical, arithmetic, bitwise, pointer. Slate so they recede; the operands matter.
Brackets and accessors — dim slate. Structural noise, dimmer than operators.

aether.lsp.path.description: Path to the aether-lsp executable. Leave empty to auto-detect (PATH, then ~/.local/bin, /usr/local/bin, and the workspace's build/aether-lsp).

aether.lsp.enable.description: Start the Aether language server when an .ae file is opened. Disable for syntax-only mode.

aether.lsp.trace.server.description: Traces the JSON-RPC traffic between VS Code and the language server.
